<!-- PROSE — edit freely in Obsidian -->

*War and Peace* (Война и мир; 1865–1869) is an epic novel by [[Leo Tolstoy]], set against the backdrop of the French invasion of Russia in 1805–1812. It follows the interconnected lives of several aristocratic families — principally the Rostovs, the Bolkonskys, and Pierre Bezukhov — through war, love, loss, and the search for meaning.

Tolstoy began writing in 1863 under the working title *1805*, originally conceiving the work as a novel about the Decembrists returning from Siberian exile. The scope expanded backward in time until it encompassed the full arc of the Napoleonic Wars. The first portions appeared serially in *Russkiy Vestnik* from 1865; the complete work was published in book form in 1869.

The novel was written entirely at [[Yasnaya Polyana]]. [[Sophia Tolstaya]] transcribed the manuscript repeatedly — by her own account, as many as seven times — as Tolstoy continually revised. The philosophical-historical essays interspersed through the narrative, culminating in the two epilogues, remain one of the work's most distinctive and debated features.

<!-- NEEDS PRIMARY SOURCE — word count, exact serial publication dates, and Sophia's transcription count should be verified against Jubilee Edition vol. 9–12 introduction and Birukoff biography -->
